results.possibleIdentity: John A. Miller is an electrical engineer and researcher who had a long career at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs), particularly at its Holmdel, New Jersey facility. He was highly active during the late 1960s and 1970s, a period marked by his doctoral studies and subsequent technical contributions. Miller received his B.S. from Gonzaga University in 1966, followed by an M.S. (1967) and a Ph.D. (1971) in Electrical Engineering from Stanford University. He joined Bell Laboratories in 1966 and transitioned to technical staff roles where his work focused on network theory and communication systems. From 1971 to 1976, he worked on exploratory projects that led to the development of the Horizon communications system, an early electronic PBX system utilizing stored program control and solid-state memory. He served as the supervisor of the Horizon design group beginning in 1976. His technical expertise in semiconductor physics (applied to solid-state memory and communication devices) and network theory align with the specialized technical profiles associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which facilitated collaborations between Bell Labs engineers and contemporary artists.
